summary: A veteran long-haul truck driver examines the growing challenges facing the OTR trucking profession, focusing on invasive surveillance through inward-facing cameras and electronic logging devices. The post details how ELDs, while preventing dangerous overdriving, create perverse incentives that can compromise driver safety and pay, and explains how drivers game these systems through loopholes. The author also exposes how 'per diem pay' schemes benefit companies at drivers' expense while obscuring true income figures. The piece concludes by acknowledging trucking's traditional advantages—low barriers to entry, autonomy, and practical benefits—while warning that automation may soon eliminate these jobs entirely.
key_points: Inward-facing cameras that constantly monitor drivers are becoming standard, with Swift Transportation leading the trend, threatening the autonomy that makes trucking appealing

Electronic logging devices create impossible choices between sacrificing pay, parking unsafely, or recording violations—leading drivers to exploit system loopholes like timing accelerations to avoid detection
Hours-of-service rules fail to prevent employers from ordering night drivers to suddenly drive all night, with no time-of-day protections after industry lobbying suspended 2013 reforms
Per diem pay schemes reduce reported trucker incomes by up to 20% while benefiting companies through lower payroll taxes and hidden administrative fees
Automation threatens to eliminate trucking jobs entirely, making the profession a poor long-term career choice for younger workers
money_quotes: That's a lot of time to be in front of a running camera, never knowing for sure who might be watching you.
Such orders went into my personal 'go ahead, fire me if you need to' (for refusing) category many years ago, but this shouldn't have been necessary.
This sweet tax deduction sours into something called per diem pay when employers decide to get in the middle and 'reimburse' drivers instead.

The reason I left my longtime, relatively well-paying OTR job five years ago wasn't so much because my employer switched over to using per diem pay, it was more because of a deceptive 'more pay in your pocket' ad campaign it foisted on drivers.

One of the standard pros people cite is you don't have a boss breathing down your neck. You do have one telling you where to go and when, but yes, the physical distance helps. Unfortunately inward-facing cameras threaten to obliterate the no-boss-breathing advantage.
Viewed strictly within the context of job-seeking my years of formal math education have served little to no useful purpose to date.
key_insight: Trucking's traditional appeal—autonomy, decent pay for accessible work, freedom from direct oversight—is being systematically eroded by surveillance technology and corporate pay schemes that shift risks onto drivers while automation looms as the final threat.
